summary:
The Treaty of Utrecht (1713–1714 AD) was the series of peace agreements that ended the [[event:2347|War of the Spanish Succession]], one of the most consequential diplomatic settlements of the 18th century, establishing a new European balance of power and marking a significant expansion of British imperial and commercial power at the expense of France and Spain. The treaties — signed at Utrecht between April and July 1713, with supplementary agreements at Rastatt and Baden in 1714 — involved most European powers and settled the complex Spanish succession question: Philip V (grandson of Louis XIV) retained Spain and its colonial empire but formally renounced any claim to the French throne, separating the Bourbon succession lines to prevent a Franco-Spanish dynastic union.

For Britain, Utrecht was a commercial and imperial triumph of the first order. Britain gained from Spain: Gibraltar and Minorca (strategic Mediterranean possessions), Newfoundland, Acadia (Nova Scotia), and Hudson Bay territories in North America, and — most lucratively — the asiento, the exclusive contract to supply 4,800 enslaved Africans annually to Spanish American colonies for 30 years, plus permission to send one annual ship of goods to trade with Spanish America. These commercial privileges, combined with territorial gains, represented a significant advance in British Atlantic commercial dominance. From France, Britain gained Acadia, Newfoundland, and the recognition of British sovereignty over the Iroquois Confederacy territories. The treaty established the principle that European powers should maintain a balance of power to prevent any single state from achieving continental hegemony — a concept that would structure European diplomacy for the following century.
